items.question_text: Complete: Provide radar services only if you are _____ that the radar presentation and equipment performance is adequate for the service being provided.
items.explanation: The paragraph uses 'personally satisfied,' which places the adequacy judgment on the individual controller providing the service.

choices.text: personally satisfied

items.question_text: A controller sees the radar display flickering but recalls the system passed its commissioning flight check last month. What is the correct assessment?
items.explanation: The controller's personal satisfaction must be current and ongoing; a past commissioning flight check does not override the present-condition judgment. The note explicitly states services are not limited to commissioning flight check parameters, but also does not say a past check excuses current inadequacy.

choices.text: The controller should continue using the radar because it passed the commissioning check.
The controller must assess current adequacy regardless of the past commissioning check and withhold radar services if not satisfied.
The commissioning check supersedes the controller's personal judgment for 12 months.
The controller may provide radar services as long as any target appears, regardless of presentation quality.

items.question_text: How does the commissioning flight check note interact with the controller's personal satisfaction requirement?
items.explanation: The note says radar service is not limited to the distance and altitude parameters from the commissioning flight check, meaning a controller may provide service beyond those parameters if satisfied. It does not mean the controller can provide service when the radar presentation is inadequate — the personal satisfaction requirement still governs.

choices.text: The note allows service beyond commissioning check parameters but does not relax the personal satisfaction requirement.
The note replaces the personal satisfaction requirement with the commissioning check parameters.
The note means radar services are always permitted within commissioning check parameters regardless of current presentation.
The note applies only to en route facilities, not terminal.

items.question_text: Complete the three conditions under which secondary radar may be used as the sole display source outside Class A airspace: (a) additional coverage is provided by secondary radar beyond that of the primary radar, (b) the primary radar is temporarily unusable or out of service and pilots are _____, or (c) _____.
items.explanation: Condition (b) requires advising pilots of the condition. Condition (c) is when a secondary radar system is the only source of radar data for the area of service.

choices.text: advised
a secondary radar system is the only source of radar data for the area of service

items.question_text: In terminal FUSION mode, the provisions of 5-1-2a do not apply. Under what condition do they apply?
items.explanation: The FUSION mode exemption from 5-1-2a is overridden when a facility directive requires it.

choices.text: When the facility has an ATIS broadcast running.
When required by facility directive.
When more than five aircraft are on frequency.
When primary radar is also available.

items.question_text: A terminal controller is asked whether secondary-radar-only targets may be used for an ASR final approach when no emergency exists. What is the correct answer?
items.explanation: Paragraph 5-1-2b states that terminal facilities shall not use secondary radar only to conduct ASR final approaches unless an emergency exists and the pilot concurs.

choices.text: Yes, if the aircraft is ADS-B equipped.
No, secondary-radar-only ASR final approaches require both an emergency and pilot concurrence.
Yes, if primary radar is temporarily unavailable.
Yes, in STARS Fusion mode only.

items.question_text: When the primary radar is temporarily unusable and the controller issues 'PRIMARY RADAR UNAVAILABLE. RADAR SERVICES AVAILABLE ON TRANSPONDER OR ADS-B EQUIPPED AIRCRAFT ONLY,' under what circumstances may this advisory be omitted?
items.explanation: The note provides two omit conditions: when the advisory is on ATIS and the pilot has the ATIS information, or when provided by other appropriate notice to pilots.

choices.text: When the outage is expected to last less than five minutes.
When provided on ATIS and the pilot indicates having that ATIS information, or when provided by other appropriate notice to pilots.
When the pilot is on an IFR flight plan.
When only one aircraft is on frequency.

items.question_text: What is the key operational difference between en route and terminal procedures for stopping EA activity when it interferes with radar use?
items.explanation: En route facilities directly request the responsible military unit or aircraft. Terminal facilities normally request suspension through the ARTCC, but may broadcast directly on the emergency frequency when immediate cessation is required.

choices.text: En route controllers must always go through a military liaison, while terminal controllers act independently.
En route requests the responsible unit or aircraft directly; terminal normally goes through the ARTCC but may broadcast directly on the emergency frequency when immediate cessation is required.
Terminal controllers may never communicate with the EA aircraft; en route controllers have no restrictions.
Both en route and terminal must always route all EA stop requests through the ARTCC.

items.question_text: What is the difference between the en route and terminal procedures for resuming previously suspended EA activity?
items.explanation: En route informs the NORAD unit or aircraft. Terminal informs the ARTCC or aircraft, but if broadcasting directly to the aircraft, must first obtain ARTCC approval.

choices.text: En route informs NORAD; terminal must contact the military unit directly.
En route informs the NORAD unit or aircraft. Terminal informs the ARTCC or aircraft but requires ARTCC approval before broadcasting directly to the aircraft.
There is no difference; both use the same procedure.
Terminal never participates in resume clearances.

items.question_text: Complete: In each stop request, include your _____, type of EA activity (chaff dispensing — 'stream'/'burst' or electronic jamming — 'buzzer'), _____ affected, and when feasible, expected duration of suspension.
items.explanation: The paragraph lists four required elements: facility name, type of EA activity, radar band affected, and (when feasible) expected duration.

choices.text: facility name
radar band

items.question_text: Which of the following aircraft is NOT subject to merging target procedures while established in a holding pattern?
items.explanation: All three covered categories (aircraft at or above 10,000 feet, turbojets regardless of altitude, and presidential aircraft regardless of altitude) are exempt from merging target procedures while established in a holding pattern. The question tests whether the student understands the holding pattern exception applies to all covered categories.

choices.text: A radar-identified turbojet at 5,000 feet established in a holding pattern.
A radar-identified piston aircraft at 10,000 feet not in a holding pattern.
A radar-identified turbojet at FL350 cruising en route.
A presidential aircraft at 12,000 feet not in a holding pattern.

items.question_text: When two aircraft covered by merging target rules are in RVSM airspace and vertically separated by exactly 1,000 feet, when must vectors be used to prevent targets from merging?
items.explanation: Vectors are required when either pilot reports being unable to maintain RVSM due to turbulence or mountain wave. The note clarifies this phrase is only intended for severe turbulence with altitude deviations of approximately 200 feet or more.

choices.text: When either pilot reports unable RVSM due to turbulence or mountain wave.
Whenever the aircraft are opposite direction regardless of pilot report.
Only when both pilots simultaneously report unable RVSM.
When the controller observes a groundspeed difference exceeding 50 knots.

items.question_text: A pilot whose traffic information was previously issued requests a vector to avoid merging targets, but the controller's frequency is saturated with higher-priority transmissions. What must the controller ultimately do?
items.explanation: Paragraph 5-1-4d authorizes vectors on pilot request, but 5-1-4e requires the controller to inform the pilot if unable to provide vector service. The obligation to inform is not contingent on reason.

choices.text: Vector the aircraft as soon as possible without informing the pilot of any delay.
Ignore the request since traffic information was already issued.
Inform the pilot that vector service cannot be provided at this time.
Terminate radar service to relieve the obligation.

items.question_text: The holding pattern surveillance rule applies to outer-fix holding pattern airspace areas shown on the scope. An aircraft is holding at an unpublished holding fix that was assigned by ATC and is not depicted on the video map. Does 5-1-5 apply?
items.explanation: The paragraph is limited to outer-fix holding pattern airspace areas shown on the radar scope (displayed on the video map or scribed on the map overlay). If the holding area is not depicted, the specific surveillance obligation under 5-1-5 does not apply.

choices.text: Yes, because any aircraft holding in any location triggers the rule.
No, the rule is limited to holding areas depicted on the video map or scribed on the map overlay.
Yes, but only if the pilot requests surveillance.
No, but only because unpublished holds are prohibited.

items.question_text: An RNAV ATS route has a total width of 8 NM. Laterally protected airspace extends 4 NM on each side. What navigation performance is an aircraft on such a route required to be capable of?
items.explanation: The note states the aircraft system must be capable of remaining within 2 NM of the route centerline. The 4 NM protected airspace per side and 2 NM performance requirement are numerically distinct and easily confused.

choices.text: Remaining within 2 NM of the route centerline.
Remaining within 4 NM of the route centerline.
Remaining within 8 NM of the route centerline.
Remaining within the total 8 NM route width on either side.

items.question_text: Under 5-1-6, a deviation advisory is issued when the aircraft is observed in a position and on a track which will _____ cause the aircraft to deviate from its protected airspace area.
items.explanation: The standard is 'obviously' — the deviation must be obvious from the observed position and track, not merely possible or speculative.

choices.text: obviously

items.question_text: A terminal controller hears about the manual fix posting requirement in 5-1-7 and begins manually recording fix times for all controlled aircraft. Is this correct?
items.explanation: 5-1-7 is explicitly scoped 'EN ROUTE' and does not apply to terminal operations. The paragraph also requires the EAS FDP flight progress recording components to be not operational as a precondition.

choices.text: Yes, manual fix posting applies to all ATC facilities whenever aircraft are being controlled.
No, 5-1-7 applies only to en route operations and only when EAS FDP flight progress recording components are not operational.
Yes, but only during hours of darkness.
No, manual fix posting applies only when radar is entirely unavailable.

items.question_text: An aircraft cancels its IFR flight plan while inside Class C airspace. The controller stops providing radar service without issuing a termination advisory. Is this correct?
items.explanation: IFR cancellation is an automatic termination case, but the paragraph explicitly EXCEPTS Class B, Class C, TRSA, and areas where basic radar service is provided. Since the aircraft is in Class C, the controller must still advise of termination.

choices.text: Yes, IFR cancellation always triggers automatic termination without advisory.
No, IFR cancellation inside Class C airspace does not trigger the automatic termination exception; the aircraft must be advised.
Yes, but only if the aircraft was VFR.
No, radar service can never be automatically terminated.

items.question_text: At a tower-controlled airport where radar coverage exists to within 0.3 miles of the runway end, an arriving IFR aircraft on an instrument approach lands. Must the controller issue a 'RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED' advisory?
items.explanation: The special advisory requirement (5-1-9b3) only applies at airports where radar coverage does NOT exist to within 1/2 mile of the runway end. Here coverage exists to 0.3 miles (within 0.5), so the exception does not apply. The automatic termination under 5-1-9b2 (aircraft on instrument approach has landed) applies, and no separate advisory is needed.

choices.text: Yes, because any landing requires a termination advisory.
No, the aircraft landed on an instrument approach, triggering automatic termination, and the coverage-exception rule does not apply because radar reaches within 0.5 miles.
Yes, because radar coverage stops at 0.3 miles, which is less than 0.5.
No, radar service continues until the aircraft parks at the gate.

items.question_text: A VFR aircraft receiving radar service to a non-tower airport is instructed to change to advisory frequency. Under 5-1-9b4, what happens to radar service?
items.explanation: 5-1-9b4 distinguishes between tower-controlled airports in Class B/C/TRSA/basic-radar-service areas (where landing triggers automatic termination) and all other airports (where being instructed to change to tower or advisory frequency triggers automatic termination). For non-tower airports, the instruction to change to advisory frequency is the trigger.

choices.text: Radar service is automatically terminated when instructed to change to advisory frequency.
Radar service continues until the aircraft reports on the ground.
The controller must issue 'RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED' before the frequency change.
Radar service continues until the pilot cancels flight following.

items.instruction: Determine the correct response for this terminal operation.
items.situation: You are working terminal radar. Primary radar fails suddenly. Secondary radar remains operational. The failure is expected to be temporary, and the advisory is already on the ATIS recording. A pilot checks in and states they have ATIS information.
items.question_text: What must you do with respect to the primary radar unavailability advisory for this pilot?

choices.text: Issue the full primary-radar-unavailable advisory because ATIS does not substitute for individual pilot notification.
Omit the advisory because it is on ATIS and the pilot indicated having the ATIS information.
Issue only 'RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED' and switch to nonradar procedures.
Ask the pilot whether they want the advisory before deciding.

items.explanation: Under 5-1-2 note, the primary-radar-unavailable advisory may be omitted when provided on ATIS and the pilot indicates having ATIS information.

items.instruction: Determine the correct response for this terminal operation.
items.situation: Terminal radar is being severely jammed by EA activity and you need the activity stopped immediately. Through the normal ARTCC path will take too long. An EA aircraft is visible on the scope and within emergency frequency range.
items.question_text: What is the correct procedure?

choices.text: Wait for the ARTCC regardless of urgency because terminal facilities may never contact EA aircraft directly.
Broadcast the stop request directly to the EA aircraft on the emergency frequency and notify the ARTCC of the direct broadcast as soon as possible.
Continue providing degraded radar service until the ARTCC acts on its own.
Request suspension through the destination tower instead of the ARTCC.

items.explanation: 5-1-3B2 permits terminal facilities to broadcast directly to the EA aircraft on the emergency frequency when immediate cessation is required, with ARTCC notification as soon as possible afterward.

items.instruction: Determine whether merging target procedures apply.
items.situation: A radar-identified piston aircraft is cruising at 9,500 feet and is not in a holding pattern. Its target appears likely to merge with another covered aircraft's target.
items.question_text: Does this piston aircraft receive merging target procedures?

choices.text: No, because it is below 10,000 feet, is not a turbojet, and is not a presidential aircraft.
Yes, because it is radar-identified and below 10,000 feet.
Yes, because any radar-identified aircraft receives merging target procedures.
No, merging target procedures only apply above FL180.

items.explanation: Merging target procedures apply to three categories: aircraft at or above 10,000 feet, all turbojets regardless of altitude, and presidential aircraft. A piston aircraft at 9,500 feet falls into none of these categories.

items.instruction: Determine whether a separate termination advisory is required.
items.situation: An aircraft cancels its IFR flight plan while inside Class B airspace.
items.question_text: Must the controller issue a separate 'RADAR SERVICE TERMINATED' advisory?

choices.text: Yes, because IFR cancellation inside Class B airspace is excepted from the automatic termination rule.
No, because IFR cancellation always triggers automatic termination without advisory.
No, because the aircraft is in controlled airspace and radar service cannot be terminated at all.
Yes, but only if the pilot specifically requests it.

items.explanation: IFR cancellation is listed as an automatic termination situation, but the paragraph explicitly excepts Class B, Class C, TRSA, and areas where basic radar service is provided. Inside Class B, the controller must still issue the termination advisory.
